subject.title: English A Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **literary texts from diverse cultures, genres, and time periods**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis, interpretation, and understanding of how literature reflects and shapes human experience through themes, cultural contexts, and global issues**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Unseen literary text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay on studied works | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 40% |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation on global issues | 10 minutes | 10 minutes | 25% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated, focused, and effectively guides the structure of the investigation.
The topic is communicated accurately through a precise title, an orienting introduction, and consistent section headings.
The methodology identifies both primary and secondary sources and applies appropriate literaryâtheoretical lenses with proper inâtext citations and a bibliography.
weaknesses: The methodology discussion lacks a critical rationale for selecting psychoanalytic theory and clear criteria for evaluating source relevance.
Some explanatory sections (e.g., on Freud and Bollas) drift into description without sustained linkage to the research question.
The conclusion extends to societal policy questions rather than focusing tightly on implications for literary interpretation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates a clear purpose and focus, with the research question tightly integrated into each section and the topic communicated effectively via title and headings. Methodologically, it is generally complete with a suitable range of sources and correct referencing, but it falls short of fully justifying its theoretical and source selections. These strengths and minor weaknesses align with a high-band performance (5/6) for Focus and Method.

strengths: Extensive and coherent knowledge of Toni Morrisonâs Beloved, including plot details, character dynamics, and historical context.
Effective integration of multiple scholarly sources to support interpretive claims about identity and trauma.
Mostly accurate and appropriate use of literary and psychoanalytic terminology to frame arguments.
weaknesses: A handful of secondary materials (e.g., some web articles) vary in academic weight and are paraphrased without deep critical engagement.
Occasional lapses in conceptual precision, such as conflating distinct psychoanalytic terms without full clarification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student exhibits excellent understanding of the novel and grounds interpretations in a solid range of sources. Terminology is used competently, though not flawlessly. Overall, these qualities correspond to a strong upper-band performance (5/6) for Knowledge and Understanding.

strengths: A broad and relevant research base that consistently ties scholarly and historical references back to the research question.
Analysis that moves beyond summary to uncover symbolic patterns and psychoanalytic dimensions, supported by close readings of textual evidence.
A logically structured argument that engages with alternative readings and theoretical perspectives.
weaknesses: Some evaluative discussion remains descriptive rather than critically interrogative, with limited counter-argument.
Source credibility is not always questioned, leading to variable scholarly rigour.
Occasional paragraphs lapse into extended narrative summary without nuanced qualification of conclusions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation demonstrates good to very good critical thinking: research is consistently relevant (sub-score 7), analysis is well supported (sub-score 6), and the argument is generally coherent with emerging evaluation (sub-score 5). Averaging these strengths and weaknesses yields an overall score of 7/12, reflecting a solid performance in Critical Thinking.

strengths: A clearly appropriate structure with all required elements (title page, contents, logical headings, body, conclusion, Works Cited).
Consistent and correct application of formatting conventions (MLA inâtext citations, page numbering, font, spacing).
weaknesses: Minor pagination discrepancies in the table of contents.
Occasional long sentences and complex paragraphs that could be streamlined for clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets all EE requirements with a coherent structure and accurate layout. Any lapses are minor and do not impede readability. This level of execution corresponds to the maximum band performance (4/4) for Presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The essay demonstrates a generally sound methodology: it identifies a primary text, articulates a literary-theoretical lens (Freud, Bollas), and incorporates a bibliography with a respectable range of secondary academic sources. In-text citations are provided and the word count is within limits.  

However, the rationale for source selection and the specific methods of literary analysis are only briefly noted rather than critically justified; there is little reflection on why psychoanalysis is the most suitable approach or on how secondary works were evaluated for relevance. Some source use skews toward general web articles alongside scholarly texts, suggesting partial (not wholly systematic) selection.  

Consequently, the methodology can be termed âmostly completeâ and âgenerally relevant,â aligning with the 3â4 band; the higher of the two marks in that band (4) is awarded because coverage and referencing practices are broadly competent despite limited methodological self-awareness.

Formatting conventionsâtitle page with subject, category and word count; table of contents; MLA-style in-text citations and Works Cited; consistent headingsâare observed and largely accurate. Page numbers are indicated in the contents list and appear on the extracts provided.  

While occasional line-break anomalies and minor citation inconsistencies occur, the overall layout is clean, readable and meets EE requirements for font, spacing and sectioning. Consequently, the strand satisfies the descriptors for complete and correct application, meriting the maximum 4 in the 3â4 band.

The research base is **broad and largely pertinent**: the student cites a range of literary critics, psychoanalytic theorists and historical references that illuminate aspects of identity and slavery in Beloved. Most sources are clearly connected to the research question and are integrated into the discussion.  

There is some variability in scholarly rigour (e.g., inclusion of popular websites alongside peer-reviewed articles), and the essay does not always interrogate source credibility, which restrains the level of excellence.  

Still, the breadth, relevance and generally appropriate use of sources reflect a good to very good level of research quality, aligning with the lower end of the 7â9 band; hence a score of 7 is assigned.

The student states the topic explicitly in the title ("Slavery Impact on Identity") and reiterates it in a focused introduction that frames the essay as an exploration of the lasting psychological and cultural effects of slavery in Toni Morrisonâs Beloved.  

â¢ The opening paragraph clearly situates the novel in its historical context and signals the lines of inquiry (identity, trauma, healing) that will be pursued.  
â¢ Throughout the essay, paragraph headings (e.g., âThe symbolism of trees,â âThe animalization of Sethe and Paul D,â âThe reconstruction of Setheâs identityâ) reinforce a sustained thematic focus.  

Minor lapses in phrasing (âTo tackle the research questionâ¦â) and some overly long sentences occasionally blur precision, but overall the topic is communicated accurately and with a consistent sense of purpose, meriting the upperâband award.

The discussion is coherent and generally well organised, advancing a clear line of argument about how slavery shapes and reconstructs identity. The student evaluates symbolism and thematic concerns, and there is some reflection on alternative readings (e.g., challenges to Fultonâs view of trees as purely redemptive).  

However, evaluative elements are uneven: counter-arguments are limited, and conclusions sometimes restate rather than critically reflect on findings. The engagement with theoretical lenses could be more interrogative, and the implications raised in the final rhetorical question are not fully explored.  

Given these strengths and shortcomings, the work aligns with the middle of the 4â6 band for discussion and evaluation; a score of 5 is warranted.

The analysis moves beyond narrative summary to probe **symbolic patterns (trees, animalisation), psychoanalytic dimensions of trauma, and communal rituals**. Close reading of textual evidence (e.g., quotations on the chokecherry tree, Paul Dâs reflections on Mister) is generally linked to interpretations about identity formation.  

Most claims are supported by textual examples and secondary scholarship, demonstrating logical reasoning. Nevertheless, some analytical threads are pursued at length without counter-argument or nuanced qualification, and a few paragraphs lapse into descriptive retelling.  

Overall, the depth and effectiveness of analysis are strong but not consistently sophisticated, fitting the top of the 4â6 band; a score of 6 is therefore appropriate.

The essay presents a **clear, conventional EE structure**: title page with word count, abstract substituted by an introduction, logical section headings, a table of contents, main body, conclusion and Works Cited. Transitions between sections are generally smooth, enabling reader navigation and understanding.  

All required elements appear to be present; any minor lapses (e.g., slightly uneven page-number references in the contents table) do not significantly impede clarity. Thus the structure meets expectations fully, justifying a mark at the top of the 3â4 band.

The student displays **extensive knowledge of Beloved**âplot details, character dynamics, symbolic motifsâand integrates contextual information (e.g., Fugitive Slave Act) to ground the discussion. A variety of scholarly sources (articles by Fulton, Bonnet, Kang, etc.) are cited and woven into the argument, generally supporting interpretive claims rather than merely padding the essay.  

Some secondary material is paraphrased without deep synthesis, and a few sources (e.g., web articles) are of mixed academic weight, preventing the highest possible mark. Nevertheless, understanding of the novelâs themes and historical background is coherent and convincingly applied, warranting a solid placement in the 5â6 band at score 5.

The essay employs a good range of literary and psychoanalytic terminologyââsymbolism,â âimagery,â âdefense mechanism,â âpre-Oedipal,â âscreen memories,â âlexical repetitionââand uses them mostly accurately. This shows more than basic familiarity with English A: Literature concepts.  

Occasionally, terms are applied somewhat mechanically or without full clarification (e.g., conflating Freudâs repression with Bollasâs screen memory, minor inaccuracies in psychoanalytic theory), indicating that conceptual precision is not wholly consistent.  

Given the generally correct yet sometimes imperfect use of specialist language, the work sits comfortably in the 3â4 band; a mark of 4 recognises the overall strength while acknowledging intermittent lapses.

The research questionââHow does Toni Morrisonâs Beloved explore the profound impacts of slavery on the identity of African Americans through her characters?ââis explicitly stated, narrowly framed around identity formation and firmly rooted in a single primary text.  

The analysis in each section returns directly to this question, and sub-headings map out the facets (trees, animalisation, communal rituals) that structure the discussion, showing a strong connection between the question and the ensuing argument.  

Some sections (e.g., psychoanalytic lens explanation) drift into descriptive summaries of theory rather than sustained linkage to the RQ, preventing a perfect âeffectiveâ rating, but the overall focus and appropriateness remain clear and purposeful.

annotations.comment: Several web-based sources are cited alongside peer-reviewed scholarship without discussion of their relative credibility; justify source selection criteria.
The title clearly communicates the investigationâs focus on slaveryâs impact on identity, providing a precise thematic anchor for the essay.
The research question is explicitly stated and narrowly framed around identity formation in Beloved, establishing a clear analytical scope.
The word count is provided on the title page, meeting the formatting requirement and demonstrating adherence to EE guidelines.
The table of contents presents clear section headings and corresponding page numbers, enhancing navigability.
Some page numbers in the contents list do not align precisely with actual sections; verify pagination to ensure accuracy.
The ghost of Beloved is interpreted cogently as unresolved trauma, enriched by Khaleghiâs scholarship on memory and resistance.
The introduction situates Beloved in its historical context and frames key themes (identity, trauma, healing) with clarity.
Several sentences are overly long and complex, which can obscure precision; consider breaking them into shorter, more focused statements.
The second paragraph repeats setting details and publication information; streamline to eliminate redundancy and enhance conciseness.
The methodology overview names theoretical lenses and sources but lacks a clear rationale for selecting psychoanalysis and criteria for source evaluation.
This sentence fragment beginning with âsuch asâ lacks a main clause; rephrase to form a complete, grammatically correct sentence.
Insightful analysis of irony in âIt never looked as terrible as it wasâ effectively links textual imagery to trauma defense mechanisms.
The paragraph ends abruptly with âchallenging societal notions of âhomeâ forâ; complete this clause to maintain coherence.
Multiple theories (Freud, Tjerngren, Fulton) are conflated without distinguishing their conceptual contributions; clarify each theoryâs unique relevance.
This lengthy paragraph would benefit from being broken into smaller thematic sections to improve readability and logical flow.
The close reading of animalistic imagery with conjunction analysis incisively illustrates dehumanization under slavery.
The metaphor ends mid-thought with âusing hummingbirds asâ; complete and link this image to your broader argument.
The analysis of repeated âNo. No. Nononoâ¦â effectively captures fracturing of Setheâs psyche and demonstrates nuanced textual engagement.
Discussion of Kangâs assertion on bestiality is descriptive; deepen by critically evaluating his claim and its implication for identity.
The metaphorical reading of communal singing as a performative rebirth powerfully underscores collective healing and identity formation.
The connection between Fellugaâs repression theory and Setheâs animalistic urination requires clearer theoretical linkage and elaboration.
Sophisticated integration of Bollasâs screen memories and Freudâs pre-Oedipal complex advances a strong discussion of identity reconstruction.
The concluding question extends beyond the literary analysis into societal policy; refocus your conclusion on implications for literary study and interpretation.
Works Cited entries show inconsistent MLA formatting (italics, punctuation, access dates); apply a uniform style for all digital and print sources.
Additional source listings at the end resemble an unnumbered appendix; integrate these into the main bibliography and maintain sequential ordering.
